success_surface: Formula generation with pattern detection from existing spreadsheet data
effective_behaviors: Two-phase exploration strategy: quick summary inspection followed by detailed formula-aware analysis when needed
Tool selection based on data sensitivity: switch from pandas to openpyxl when formulas must be preserved or inspected
Leverage existing cell content as pattern hints: examine target area for pre-existing formulas or values that indicate expected behavior
Discover data distribution before implementing logic: scan source columns to find actual data locations rather than assuming positions
Programmatic formula construction with verification loop: build formulas dynamically while confirming each application before save
Constraint-bound modification: respect answer_position range limits without modifying untargeted cells
Post-modification validation: verify applied cell values programmatically before finalizing workbook
evidence: First used pandas for structural overview (sheet names, dtypes, shape), then switched to openpyxl when formulas were needed
Examined column C existing formulas (=A25, =A41) which revealed the intended pattern before writing solution
Scanned column A for non-None values across all rows to discover data at rows 25, 41, 57, 73, 89
Used for loop with calculated offsets to apply formulas across C2:C6 range consistently
Print verification output showing each cell's formula value before wb.save()
Modified only the specified answer_position C2:C6, leaving other cells unchanged

memory_items.title: Formula-Aware Inspection Escalation
memory_items.lesson: When cell formulas matter, upgrade from summary tools (pandas) to direct cell access (openpyxl) after initial exploration shows ambiguity
memory_items.generalization: For any task involving formulas, styles, or merged cells, always include an openpyxl-based verification phase before implementing modifications

memory_items.title: Pattern Inference from Existing Content
memory_items.lesson: Existing formulas or values in the target region often reveal the expected transformation pattern
memory_items.generalization: Before writing new logic, scan the answer_position area for any pre-existing data, formulas, or values that indicate the intended result structure

memory_items.title: Source Data Distribution Discovery
memory_items.lesson: Assume nothing about where data exists—scan the full relevant column to find actual populated rows
memory_items.generalization: When referencing source columns, iterate through the complete column range to identify all non-empty cells rather than relying on header information alone

memory_items.title: Verification-Before-Save Protocol
memory_items.lesson: Apply and validate modifications in-memory before persisting to disk
memory_items.generalization: After bulk formula/value application, read back critical cells programmatically to confirm correctness before calling save()

memory_items.title: Dynamic Formula Construction via Loop
memory_items.lesson: Use iteration with calculated row/column indices for repetitive formula patterns instead of hardcoding each reference
memory_items.generalization: For nth-row patterns or sequential ranges, generate formulas using arithmetic expressions on row indices rather than manual enumeration

edits.content:
## Trace-Derived Lessons

### Pattern-Discovery Workflow
- When formulas must be created or referenced, inspect both target and source areas for existing patterns before writing logic
- Scan entire columns/rows for actual data presence; don't assume row positions from metadata alone
- Use existing formulas in target area as clues for expected transformation behavior

### Tool Escalation Strategy
- Start with high-level inspection (pandas) for sheet structure and column summaries
- Escalate to direct cell access (openpyxl) when formulas, exact values, or formatting details become relevant
- Always verify formula integrity in openpyxl since pandas silently converts them to values

### Verification Discipline
- After applying formulas or values to multiple cells, print or log a subset of results to confirm correctness
- Verify formula syntax matches Excel notation before saving
- Read back modified cells to ensure the stored value is the formula string, not evaluated result

### Range Constraint Adherence
- Respect answer_position boundaries strictly—only modify specified cell ranges
- Use loops with calculated offsets for patterned modifications rather than enumerating each cell individually
